summary: 8a has two distinct sections. First, the kal v'chomer from tzitz to tefillin is completed, and then R. Yehuda and R. Shimon's source derivations for their positions on the tzitz are traced verse by verse: where each derives the forehead-location requirement, where each derives what happens with a broken tzitz. Second, a new tannaitic dispute about sprinkling (hazaah) for the High Priest on Yom Kippur and the priest who burns the Red Heifer (para aduma): R. Meir says both get sprinkled all seven days; R. Yosei says only day 3 and day 7; R. Chanina Segan says para priest gets all 7, YK kohein gadol gets only day 3 and 7. The Gemara investigates whether this dispute maps onto the hutrah/dchuya debate - initially seems so, but ultimately concludes both later tanaim accept dchuya; their dispute is whether hazaah timing follows the same 'mitzva at its proper time' rule as tevila. Multiple resolution attempts are made before the correct one is identified.

display.title: Kal v'chomer to tefillin; source derivations for R. Yehuda and R. Shimon
display.oneLine: The tefillin kal v'chomer from 7b is completed; then R. Yehuda and R. Shimon each explain where they derive the forehead-location rule and what to do with a broken tzitz.
display.shortSummary: The kal v'chomer: just as the tzitz - which has only one divine name - requires constant mindfulness (not diverting attention), tefillin - which have many divine names - certainly require constant mindfulness. Then the Gemara traces how each side derives its proof-texts. R. Shimon: 'al mitzcho' establishes the required location; 'tamid liratzon' means perpetual atonement even off-forehead. R. Yehuda: 'al mitzcho' likewise establishes location; but 'tamid' means mindfulness. Question: if R. Shimon already derives location from 'al mitzcho,' what does 'al mitzcho v'nasa' add? Answer: 'ראוי למצח' - only what is fit for the forehead atones; a broken tzitz is not fit. Question: where does R. Yehuda derive that a broken tzitz does not atone? From the doubling 'mitzcho / mitzcho' in two verses (two mentions = two requirements). R. Shimon: the doubling does not convey that teaching.
display.whats: This section is source-analysis (limud): once two positions are established in 7b, the Gemara asks 'where does each derive this from?' It is not a new dispute but a derivational audit. The key technical concept is 'ראוי למצח' - the principle that atonement requires the object to be fit for its designated location, even if not currently there.
display.hint: R. Shimon and R. Yehuda each derive location from 'al mitzcho.' Their disagreement is only about what 'tamid' and 'v'nasa' add to the verse.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Gemara spend so much effort tracing each opinion's source derivation?
learning.coreTension: Both R. Yehuda and R. Shimon agree on the basic verse 'al mitzcho v'nasa.' Their dispute about off-forehead atonement must therefore come from different readings of specific words. The Gemara works backward from the known positions to find which word carries which reading - a kind of reverse-engineering of the tannaitic hermeneutics.
learning.coreMove: The principle 'ראוי למצח' (fit for the forehead) is R. Shimon's solution to the broken-tzitz case without needing an extra derivation. R. Yehuda needs an additional derivation (doubled 'mitzcho') for the same conclusion.
learning.resolution: Both R. Yehuda and R. Shimon agree: broken tzitz does not atone. They derive this from different parts of the verses. Both agree: tzitz on the forehead definitely atones. Their dispute is only about the peg case.

takeaway.text: When two tanaim reach the same conclusion (broken tzitz does not atone) but from different sources, that tells you something about how they read the verses - and by extension, what other conclusions they might draw differently from the same texts.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara often works: position known -> find the verse -> explain how each side reads it. This is not circular - the verses are there to be read, and the positions are the outputs of reading them.

learning.ahaMoment: 'ראוי למצח' - fit for the forehead - is a general principle that elegantly explains the broken-tzitz ruling without needing an additional verse. If something is not fit to occupy its halakhically designated location, it cannot perform its halakhic function from elsewhere.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think R. Yehuda and R. Shimon are arguing about different things in s01 vs. s02. They are not - the verse-analysis in s01 explains the same dispute identified by Abaye in 7b.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Yehuda: two 'mitzcho' mentions = broken tzitz rule. R. Shimon: 'ראוי למצח' already implies it. The difference: R. Yehuda needs two words; R. Shimon derives it logically from one.

argumentFlow.label: Kal v'chomer from tzitz to tefillin completed
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Just as the tzitz - which contains only one mention of God's name - requires the kohein to maintain constant mindfulness and not be distracted, tefillin - which contain many mentions of God's name - certainly require constant mindfulness.

argumentFlow.label: R. Shimon: 'al mitzcho' for location; 'tamid' for perpetual atonement
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Shimon derives the forehead-location requirement from 'al mitzcho' (on his forehead). He reads 'tamid liratzon' as the clause establishing perpetual atonement even when the tzitz is not on the forehead.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yehuda: 'al mitzcho' for location; 'tamid' for mindfulness
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Yehuda also derives location from 'al mitzcho.' He reads 'tamid' not as 'perpetual atonement' but as a behavioral requirement: do not divert attention from the tzitz (paralleling the tefillin mindfulness rule).

argumentFlow.label: What does 'al mitzcho v'nasa' (the full phrase) teach, according to R. Shimon?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If R. Shimon already derives location from 'al mitzcho' alone, what is added by the full phrase 'al mitzcho v'nasa' (on his forehead, and he shall bear)?

argumentFlow.label: R. Shimon: 'v'nasa' teaches 'ראוי למצח' - fit for the forehead
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara (R. Shimon)
argumentFlow.text: The phrase 'ראוי למצח' (fit for the forehead) - only something that is fit to be on the forehead effects atonement. A broken tzitz is no longer fit for the forehead and therefore does not atone.

argumentFlow.label: Where does R. Yehuda derive the broken-tzitz ruling?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If R. Yehuda reads 'v'nasa' differently (not for the 'ראוי' principle), from where does he derive that a broken tzitz does not atone?

sourceRefs.note: Question about R. Yehuda's source for broken-tzitz ruling

argumentFlow.label: R. Yehuda: doubled 'mitzcho/mitzcho' in two verses
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara (R. Yehuda)
argumentFlow.text: R. Yehuda derives it from the double mention of 'mitzcho' across two verses (Exodus 28 and 29), which teaches that the tzitz must be whole and actually on the forehead. R. Shimon: that doubling does not convey the broken-tzitz rule.

concepts.definition: Fit for the forehead - the principle that for the tzitz to atone, it must be in a state fit to be worn on the forehead (i.e., not broken). Derived from 'v'nasa' in the tzitz verse.
A fortiori inference: if the lighter case (tzitz, one divine name) carries a rule (mindfulness), the heavier case (tefillin, many names) certainly does.

visualizableElements.label: Verse dissection diagram
visualizableElements.description: The full tzitz verse annotated: 'al mitzcho' (location) | 'v'nasa' (rauy principle or extra verse) | 'tamid liratzon' (R. Shimon: perpetual atonement; R. Yehuda: mindfulness requirement). Color-coded by which tana reads which phrase as which rule.

visualizableElements.label: R. Yehuda vs. R. Shimon derivation table
visualizableElements.description: Two-column table: R. Yehuda's derivation sources vs. R. Shimon's. Both derive location from 'al mitzcho.' They differ on 'v'nasa' (ראוי vs. unused) and 'tamid' (mindfulness vs. perpetual atonement).

quizSeeds.question: What does 'ראוי למצח' mean and where is it derived?
quizSeeds.answer: Only what is fit for the forehead atones. Derived by R. Shimon from 'al mitzcho v'nasa' - the addition of 'v'nasa' teaches that the tzitz must be fit to be on the forehead.

quizSeeds.question: If both R. Yehuda and R. Shimon derive the forehead-location requirement from 'al mitzcho,' what are they disagreeing about?
quizSeeds.answer: What the other words in the verse teach: R. Shimon reads 'tamid liratzon' as perpetual atonement; R. Yehuda reads 'tamid' as mindfulness. And for the broken-tzitz ruling: R. Shimon uses 'ראוי למצח'; R. Yehuda uses doubled 'mitzcho.'

misconceptions.misconception: R. Shimon and R. Yehuda are deriving completely different things from completely different verses.
misconceptions.correction: They both derive location from the same word ('al mitzcho'). Their disagreement is about what the additional words - 'v'nasa' and 'tamid' - each teach.

relatedSugyot.reason: Abaye's framework from 7b is the basis for the verse-analysis here
The same daf continues with a new tannaitic dispute about hazaah

requiresUnderstanding: The tzitz verse and both tanaim's positions from 7b
How kal v'chomer works as a logical form

display.title: R. Meir/R. Yosei/R. Chanina Segan: hazaah all-7 or only day-3-and-7; tevila bizmana mitzva
display.oneLine: The sprinkling dispute among three tanaim initially seems to be about dchuya vs. hutrah, but the Gemara works through two false resolutions before concluding: both R. Meir and R. Yosei accept dchuya; their dispute is whether hazaah must happen on its halakhically designated day (like tevila bizmana mitzva) or not.
display.shortSummary: A baraita: for both the YK kohein gadol and the para-aduma priest, we sprinkle (hazaah) on all seven days of their separation (R. Meir) vs. only day 3 and day 7 (R. Yosei). R. Chanina Segan has a split position: para priest = all 7 days; YK kohein gadol = only day 3 and 7. First Gemara hypothesis: this dispute maps onto dchuya (R. Meir) vs. hutrah (R. Yosei). But this is rejected: if R. Yosei held hutrah, he would say no sprinkling at all - why bother? Second hypothesis: both hold dchuya; their dispute is about 'tevila bizmana mitzva' (is immersion at its proper time a mitzva?). R. Meir says yes (tevila bizmana mitzva), so we derive hazaah timing from tevila and spray every possible day. R. Yosei says no (tevila bizmana is not a mitzva). But this is also rejected: we know from Kiddushin and Shabbat that R. Yosei himself holds tevila bizmana IS a mitzva! Third and correct resolution: both tanaim hold tevila bizmana is a mitzva; their dispute is whether we equate hazaah to tevila. R. Meir: yes, equate (hazaah must happen on its proper day = every possible day). R. Yosei: no, don't equate (hazaah has no mitzva-at-proper-time rule). R. Chanina Segan splits: does not equate hazaah to tevila at all; para priest sprinkled all 7 for other reasons (elevated stringency); YK kohein gadol only day 3 and 7.
display.whats: This sugya is a masterclass in the Gemara's method of hypothesis and refutation. Each proposed resolution of the tannaitic dispute is shown to be internally inconsistent before the correct one is found. The final resolution is subtle: the dispute is not about dchuya/hutrah (a major point) but about a secondary halachic principle - whether hazaah inherits tevila's timing-mitzva status.
display.hint: The key to unlocking the sugya: R. Yosei is shown elsewhere to hold 'tevila bizmana mitzva' - so the second hypothesis (that R. Yosei denies this) must be wrong. The real dispute must be about something R. Yosei can hold differently without contradicting himself.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Gemara need three attempts to explain the tannaitic dispute? What went wrong with the first two?
learning.coreTension: The first hypothesis (dchuya vs. hutrah) fails because R. Yosei's position is too weak if he held hutrah - he would say no hazaah at all. The second hypothesis (tevila bizmana mitzva) fails because R. Yosei is documented elsewhere to hold the opposite. The correct resolution must explain R. Yosei's position using something he can consistently hold.
learning.coreMove: The correct resolution distinguishes two questions: (1) Is tevila bizmana a mitzva? Both agree: yes. (2) Do we equate hazaah to tevila for the timing-mitzva rule? R. Meir: yes. R. Yosei: no. These are separate questions, so R. Yosei can hold 'yes' to (1) and 'no' to (2) without contradiction.
learning.resolution: The halakha follows R. Yosei's position (hazaah only on day 3 and day 7) for the YK kohein gadol, and R. Chanina Segan's ruling for the para priest distinction. The practical implications are worked out in 8b.

takeaway.text: When the Gemara rejects a hypothesis, look carefully at WHY it was rejected. Each rejection adds a constraint on what the correct resolution must look like. By the time all false resolutions are eliminated, the remaining answer is often precise and surprising.

reasoningPattern.notes: Classic Talmudic triple-hypothesis structure: (1) broad claim (dchuya/hutrah) rejected by consequence; (2) narrower claim (tevila mitzva) rejected by external source; (3) correct resolution (equating hazaah to tevila) that avoids both previous problems.

learning.ahaMoment: R. Yosei is shown to hold 'tevila bizmana mitzva' from a completely different context (Tractate Kiddushin: a person with God's name tattooed on his skin, who wraps reed to immerse). This cross-tractate citation is what forces the Gemara to abandon its second hypothesis and find the real dispute.
learning.learningBlocker: Students often think 'tevila bizmana mitzva' is the whole dispute. It is not - that's the rejected second hypothesis. The actual dispute is one level more subtle: whether hazaah is equated to tevila for timing purposes.
learning.memoryAnchor: Three tanaim, three positions: R. Meir (all 7 days, both), R. Yosei (day 3 and 7, both), R. Chanina Segan (split: para = all 7, YK = day 3 and 7). Resolution: both R. Meir and R. Yosei accept dchuya; they dispute whether hazaah inherits tevila's timing-mitzva rule.

argumentFlow.label: Three-way tannaitic dispute on hazaah days
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: For both the YK kohein gadol and the para priest: R. Meir says we sprinkle on all seven days of separation. R. Yosei says only day 3 and day 7. R. Chanina Segan says the para priest gets all 7, the YK kohein gadol gets only day 3 and 7.

argumentFlow.label: First hypothesis: dispute maps onto dchuya vs. hutrah
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Perhaps R. Meir holds dchuya (tumah is merely overridden; need real purity preparation = sprinkle all 7 days as a precaution) and R. Yosei holds hutrah (tumah fully permitted; minimal requirement only).

argumentFlow.label: First hypothesis rejected: if R. Yosei holds hutrah, why any hazaah?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If R. Yosei held hutrah (tumah fully permitted), why would he require hazaah at all on day 3 and day 7? He should say no hazaah is needed. The fact that he requires some hazaah proves he holds dchuya (the prohibition exists; we just override it; we must do at least the minimum).

argumentFlow.label: Second hypothesis: dispute is about tevila bizmana mitzva
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Both tanaim hold dchuya. Their dispute: is immersion at its halakhically designated time a mitzva? R. Meir: yes (tevila bizmana mitzva); therefore hazaah is also required on its proper day - and since any day might be the proper one, sprinkle all 7. R. Yosei: no (not a mitzva); sprinkle only on day 3 and 7 as the Torah specifies.

argumentFlow.label: Second hypothesis rejected: R. Yosei elsewhere holds tevila bizmana IS a mitzva
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: From another context (a person with God's name tattooed who wraps reed to immerse on Yom Kippur without rubbing), we know R. Yosei holds that immersion on time IS a mitzva. So R. Yosei cannot be disputing this.

sourceRefs.note: Second hypothesis rejected by cross-tractate source

argumentFlow.label: True resolution: dispute is whether hazaah is equated to tevila for timing
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Both tanaim: (1) hold dchuya; and (2) hold tevila bizmana mitzva. Their dispute is: do we equate hazaah to tevila for the timing-mitzva rule? R. Meir: yes - sprinkle every possible day because each day might be the prescribed day. R. Yosei: no - hazaah is not governed by the same timing rule as tevila; sprinkle only day 3 and 7 as the Torah says.

sourceRefs.note: Correct resolution: equating hazaah to tevila

argumentFlow.label: R. Chanina Segan's split position explained
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Chanina Segan does not equate hazaah to tevila at all. He holds that the para priest receives all-7-day sprinkling as a special elevated stringency (maalah b'alma), not because of the timing-mitzva equation. The YK kohein gadol, by contrast, gets only the Torah-required day 3 and 7.

concepts.definition: Ritual sprinkling of purification water mixed with para aduma (Red Heifer) ash, performed on day 3 and day 7 after corpse impurity. The question here is whether additional sprinklings are required.
The principle that immersion performed at its halakhically designated time (the seventh day of purification) is itself a mitzva, not merely a prerequisite. Both R. Meir and R. Yosei accept this.
The Red Heifer, whose ash mixed with water was used to purify those who had contracted corpse impurity. The priest who burned the heifer also required a special separation and purification.

visualizableElements.label: 7-day separation with sprinkling days
visualizableElements.description: Horizontal timeline of 7 days. R. Meir: sprinkle every day. R. Yosei: sprinkle only day 3 and day 7. R. Chanina Segan: split depending on which priest. Show why day 4 is problematic (not possibly day 3 or day 7 of tumah).

visualizableElements.label: Three-way tannaitic dispute table
visualizableElements.description: Three-column table: R. Meir (all 7, both), R. Yosei (day 3+7, both), R. Chanina Segan (split). Below each: their underlying rationale as resolved by the Gemara.

quizSeeds.question: Why is the first hypothesis (dchuya vs. hutrah) rejected for the R. Meir/R. Yosei dispute?
quizSeeds.answer: If R. Yosei held hutrah (tumah fully permitted), he would require no hazaah at all. But he does require day 3 and day 7, proving he holds dchuya.

quizSeeds.question: What is the correct explanation of why R. Meir and R. Yosei dispute how many days to sprinkle?
quizSeeds.answer: Both accept dchuya and both accept tevila bizmana mitzva. Their dispute is whether hazaah is equated to tevila for the timing-mitzva rule: R. Meir says yes (sprinkle every day); R. Yosei says no (only day 3 and 7).

quizSeeds.question: How does R. Chanina Segan's position differ from both R. Meir and R. Yosei?
quizSeeds.answer: R. Chanina Segan does not equate hazaah to tevila at all. He assigns the all-7-day sprinkling to the para priest as an elevated stringency, while the YK kohein gadol gets only the Torah's minimum (day 3 and 7).

misconceptions.misconception: R. Meir holds dchuya and R. Yosei holds hutrah in this dispute.
misconceptions.correction: Both hold dchuya - the Gemara explicitly rejects the dchuya/hutrah mapping. Their dispute is about whether hazaah timing follows the same 'bizmana mitzva' rule as tevila.

misconceptions.misconception: R. Yosei holds that 'tevila bizmana' is not a mitzva.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara shows from another context (tattooed immersion) that R. Yosei does hold 'tevila bizmana mitzva.' The correct dispute is about equating hazaah to tevila, not about tevila itself.

relatedSugyot.reason: 8b continues with the practical ruling for the YK kohein gadol separation calendar
Earlier discussion of tumah and immersion in the context of the YK kohein gadol's preparation

sugyot.finalRuling: Halakha follows the three-way dispute's correct reading: dchuya is accepted; hazaah for the para priest and for the YK kohein gadol are governed by their respective rules as worked out in 8b.
requiresUnderstanding: The dchuya vs. hutrah framework for tum'ah in public offerings
What hazaah (sprinkling) is and when it is required

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi on 'one this and one that' (echad zeh v'echad zeh): meaning both the YK kohein gadol and the para priest - sprinkle on all 7 days of separation, on day 3 and day 7 of each week of the separation period.
Rashi: 'day 3 and day 7 refers to the days of the priest's separation' - the counting begins from when he was sequestered. R. Chanina Segan's para priest ruling is explained below.

Rashi: 'R. Meir holds dchuya in tzibbur' - because real purity is required even for communal offerings, every day potentially qualifies as day 3 or day 7 from the priest's last tumah, so we sprinkle each day as a precaution.

Rashi: 'and R. Yosei holds hutrah in tzibbur' - he holds tumah is fully permitted for communal offerings, so only the minimum Torah-mandated sprinklings (day 3 and 7) are required as some elevated care, not because dchuya requires real purity.

Rashi: 'we are strict only as an elevated level' - R. Yosei's day 3 and 7 requirement is a rabbinic elevation (maalah), not a full purity requirement. He holds that tumah is hutrah and no sprinkling is strictly necessary.
Rashi: 'hazaah, why is it needed at all?' - if R. Yosei holds hutrah (tumah is fully permitted), why require any sprinkling on day 3 and 7? The Gemara is challenging the first hypothesis.

Rashi: 'derived from the Milu'im (installation offerings)' - the requirement that even for communal offerings we must use elevated care derives from the Milu'im procedure at the Tabernacle's inauguration.

Rashi on 'tevila bizmana mitzva' - it is written (Numbers 19): 'And he shall purify himself on the seventh day, and wash his garments and bathe.' This teaches that immersion is a mitzva specifically on the seventh day.

Rashi continues the verse derivation: 'and bathe' - this additional phrase teaches that immersion on the seventh day is itself a mitzva, not merely a cleansing act. And we equate hazaah to tevila: therefore hazaah timing is also a mitzva, requiring sprinkle each day.

Rashi: 'each day we sprinkle, perhaps today is the right day' - the logic of R. Meir's all-7-day sprinkle: since we don't know exactly when the priest last had tumah, every day could potentially be day 3 or day 7 of tumah, so we sprinkle as a safeguard.

Rashi: 'R. Yosei does not hold tevila bizmana mitzva' - this is the second hypothesis: R. Yosei denies that immersion at its proper time is itself a mitzva. He learns the verse differently (see Kiddushin).

Rashi: 'he shall not wash' - about one who has God's name written on his skin, the Torah says he may not wash, rub, or stand in a dirty place. But if he needs to immerse for purity, what should he do?

Rashi: 'if an obligatory immersion occurred' - if the man with the divine name tattooed needs to immerse for ritual purity (a mitzva), he wraps reed around the inscription to protect it from water. This shows he must immerse on the designated day.

Rashi: 'he goes down and immerses normally' - R. Yosei's ruling: the tattooed person may immerse without a wrapper because rubbing is the actual prohibition, not mere contact with water. And we know from this that R. Yosei holds tevila bizmana mitzva - he must immerse on the day itself.

Rashi: 'and we maintain that in tevila bizmana mitzva they disagree' - this cross-tractate source (Tractate Shabbat) confirms R. Yosei holds tevila bizmana IS a mitzva. The second hypothesis about the tannaitic dispute is therefore wrong.

Rashi: 'and here [the tanaim] dispute about this' - the correct resolution is about comparing hazaah to tevila for timing purposes. R. Meir equates hazaah to tevila (timing is a mitzva for both); R. Yosei does not equate them.
Rashi on 'we equate hazaah to tevila' - R. Meir: just as tevila must be at its proper time (a mitzva), so too hazaah must be at its proper time. Since any day might be the right day, sprinkle all 7.
Rashi: 'R. Yosei holds we do not equate hazaah to tevila' - hazaah has its own rule (day 3 and day 7 only, as the Torah specifies). It does not inherit the 'bizmana mitzva' property from tevila.
Rashi: 'which is learned in Kiddushin chapter 3' - the cross-tractate source proving R. Yosei's position on tevila bizmana is cited explicitly from Tractate Kiddushin, chapter 3.
Rashi: 'he shall not wash' - repeats the prohibition on the tattooed person against washing in a way that would rub the divine name.

Rashi on 'a mitzva immersion that presented itself' - an obligatory immersion (like a niddah or zavah immersing for purity) that the tattooed person needs to perform. 'Nizmadah' means it arose or became necessary.
Rashi: 'wraps reed around it' - he covers the tattooed area with a reed or cloth to protect the divine name from water contact during immersion, since 'not rubbing' is the key condition.

Rashi: 'to protect against the force of the water's current' - the reed wrapping is needed because the force of flowing water could rub the skin. Without the reed, the current itself might constitute rubbing.

Rashi: 'R. Yosei says: he goes down and immerses in his normal way' - R. Yosei holds that 'not rubbing' is the actual prohibition, not any contact with water. Normal immersion without rubbing is permitted even without a wrapper.

Rashi: 'and we maintain that in tevila bizmana mitzva they dispute' - the Tractate Shabbat (120b) context confirms this is the underlying dispute. R. Yosei holds tevila bizmana IS a mitzva, refuting the second hypothesis.
Rashi: 'and here the tanaim dispute about this' - the actual dispute is: R. Meir holds we equate hazaah to tevila (both must happen bizmana); R. Yosei holds we do not equate them.

Rashi: 'R. Meir holds we equate hazaah to tevila' - since tevila bizmana is a mitzva (both agree), and hazaah is equated to tevila, hazaah is also a mitzva at its proper time - which is why we sprinkle every possible day.

Rashi: 'R. Yosei holds we do not equate hazaah to tevila' - the verse 'vichitei bayom hashevi'i' (Numbers 19) teaches that tevila has a timing-mitzva property. But this is not extended to hazaah, which has its own verses specifying only day 3 and day 7.

Rashi: 'we do not equate' - hazaah's verses say 'third and seventh day' - meaning: if done on those days, valid; if done earlier or later, not valid. But between the two valid days, there is no obligation to repeat. So R. Yosei: sprinkle only day 3 and day 7.
Rashi on 'hazaah is not valid early or delayed' - if hazaah falls before day 3 or after day 7, it is invalid. But within the permitted window, it need not be done every day (per R. Yosei's reading).

Rashi: 'but if delayed substantially, there is no issue' - R. Yosei reads the verse as fixing the valid days (3 and 7), not as creating a mitzva to sprinkle on each intermediate day. Delay between the two specified days is fine.

Rashi: 'R. Chanina Segan does not equate at all' - R. Chanina Segan rejects the hazaah-tevila comparison entirely. The para priest's all-7-day sprinkling is a special elevated stringency applied to that particular role.

Rashi: 'if he equates hazaah to tevila, even the YK kohein gadol should get all-7 sprinklings' - but R. Chanina Segan says no. And if he does not equate, even the para priest should get only day 3 and 7. The split position requires a third explanation.

Rashi: 'actually R. Chanina Segan does not equate' - the resolution is that R. Chanina Segan never equates hazaah to tevila; the para priest's all-7-day sprinkling is a maalah (elevated stringency) applied because the para priest's purity is treated with extra care.

Rashi: 'the priest who burns the para aduma is an elevated level (maalah) in the world' - R. Chanina Segan holds that the para priest receives extra care not because of any equating principle but because of the special stringencies associated with burning the para aduma.
Rashi: 'who agrees with this ruling that teaches?' - the Gemara asks whose position among the earlier tanaim (R. Meir, R. Yosei) the following baraita aligns with.

Rashi: 'there is no difference between the priest who burns the para and the High Priest on Yom Kippur' - the baraita states the only distinction is in the type of separation (for purity vs. for holiness). This is used to identify which tannaitic position the baraita follows.

Rashi: 'his separation is for holiness' - the YK kohein gadol's separation is described as being for kedushah (holiness); the para priest's separation is for taharah (purity). This distinction explains why other priests may or may not touch them.

Rashi: 'his separation is for purity' - the para priest is separated so that no one touches him, because he is being elevated to a strict state of purity. This contrasts with the YK kohein gadol where the separation is for holiness, not purity.

Rashi: 'this follows either R. Meir or R. Yosei' - since both R. Meir and R. Yosei agree on the basic dichotomy between para priest and YK kohein gadol, the baraita aligns with either of them (not specifically with R. Chanina Segan, who has a more elaborate split).
Rashi: continuation into 8b - 'that this one's separation is for holiness and his fellow priests touch him' - the baraita's second half is carried over to 8b where it is analyzed further.

glossary.en: Fit for the forehead - the principle that the tzitz must be in a condition suitable for being worn on the forehead to effect atonement.
Sprinkling - the purification ritual of sprinkling water mixed with para aduma (Red Heifer) ash on someone with corpse-impurity.
Immersion at its designated time is itself a mitzva - the principle that purification immersion performed on its prescribed day (the seventh day) carries independent religious significance.
